title: Resetless Valid-Masked Register File
motivation:
The current best core already exceeds the CoreMark throughput target
substantially: 577.76 iter/s versus the required 400 iter/s. The failing
axis is area, with 5944 LUT4 against the 3500 LUT4 target, so this
micro_opt slot should target a localized LUT/FF reduction that does not
change the pipeline schedule.

The register file is still a 32 x 32 array that synchronously resets every
physical data bit. That reset loop prevents the cheapest resetless LUTRAM
mapping and adds reset muxing/fanout in the decode read region. Software and
the existing tests only require nonzero registers to read as zero after
reset until written; a small valid mask can provide that behavior while the
data payload itself becomes resetless.
hypothesis:
Replace the reset-cleared register data array in `rtl/reg_file.sv` with
resetless storage plus a resettable valid mask for x1 through x31. On reset,
clear only the valid bits. On a write to a nonzero architectural register,
update the resetless data payload and set the corresponding valid bit. Reads
preserve the current externally visible behavior: x0 always returns zero,
same-cycle writeback to a read address returns `w_data`, valid nonzero
registers return stored data, and invalid nonzero registers return zero.

Implement the storage in a synthesis-friendly shape for the FPGA, for
example two duplicated resetless read banks with one synchronous write to
both banks, so the two combinational read ports can map to distributed RAM
instead of resettable flip-flops. Avoid adding a subtractor or broad decode
on the read path; the valid mask should be indexed directly by the
architectural register number with x0 handled as a special zero case.

This changes no ISA behavior, forwarding priority, load-use interlock,
branch prediction, divider protocol, memory ordering, RVFI field, retirement
order, or top-level IO. CoreMark cycles should be identical. The intended
win is a large LUT4 and FF/reset-fanout reduction on the area axis, with
fitness staying within the current throughput headroom even if the resetless
RAM read path costs a little Fmax.

changes.description:
Remove the reset loop over the 32 x 32 data payload. Add a resettable
valid mask for nonzero registers and keep the data arrays resetless,
optionally as two mirrored read banks to encourage distributed-RAM
inference for the two asynchronous read ports. Preserve the module
interface, x0 hardwiring, nonzero write gating, and write-first bypass
behavior. Reads must check x0 and same-cycle writeback before consulting
the valid mask; invalid nonzero reads return zero without exposing
uninitialized resetless payload bits.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## What Changed

- Reworked `rtl/reg_file.sv` so reset clears only a 32-bit architectural valid mask.
- Made the register data payload resetless and duplicated it into two mirrored read banks, one per combinational read port.
- Writes to nonzero architectural registers update both payload banks and set `valid_q[w_addr]`.
- Reads still prioritize x0, then same-cycle writeback bypass, then valid stored data; invalid nonzero registers return zero without exposing resetless payload bits.

## Deviations

- I used a 32-bit valid mask indexed directly by architectural register number. Bit 0 is never set because x0 writes are dropped and x0 reads return zero before the valid check. This avoids subtract/index remapping on the read path at the cost of one unused valid bit.
- Added a `ram_style = "distributed"` attribute on both read banks as a synthesis hint; the implementation does not rely on the attribute for correctness.

## Concerns

- No known functional concerns. The change is limited to the register file and keeps the module interface, write-first bypass, x0 behavior, pipeline schedule, and RVFI-visible values unchanged.
- A direct `bash formal/run_all.sh` in this worktree failed before generating checks because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a protected shared checkout and the script could not remove staged files there (`Operation not permitted`). I ran the same script from a writable `/tmp/rf_formal.NTpDRb` mirror with the current RTL symlinked in.

## Local Status

- Lint: pass, `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`.
- Focused unit test: pass, `pytest -q test/test_reg_file.py`.
- Formal: pass from the writable mirror, `Formal: 105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: Remove IF replay table
motivation: LUT4 is the only failing axis: the current design reaches 577.76 iter/s against a 400 iter/s CoreMark target, but uses 5944 LUT4 against a 3500 LUT4 target. The largest clearly structural area feature in the RTL is the two-bank 128-entry instruction replay table in IF. It stores instructions, tags, valid bits, MRU state, and hit/bypass comparators to mask random imem stalls, even though the current CoreMark margin is large enough to trade some stall-hiding for a major area reduction.
hypothesis: Replace the IF replay-table front end with a bare PC fetch stage that keeps the existing static backward-branch/direct-JAL prediction but treats io_imemReady=0 as a normal fetch stall. Removing the replay arrays and their lookup/fill control should cut a large block of LUTRAM/FF/comparator logic and may also improve Fmax by shortening IF-side control. CoreMark/MHz will drop because random imem stalls are no longer hidden, but the baseline has enough performance headroom that the design should remain above the 400 iter/s target while moving LUT4 much closer to the 3500 target.

changes.description: Delete the replay-table arrays, tag/MRU state, lookup/fill/bypass logic, and replay-current-hit path; drive fetch_ready directly from imem_ready while preserving redirect priority, PC hold behavior, NOP-on-flush, and static branch/JAL prediction from the live instruction word.
Update the IF/ID bundle comments to remove replay-table-specific documentation while keeping the predicted_taken and predicted_target fields unchanged.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## What changed

- Replaced the IF replay-table front end with a bare PC fetch stage in `rtl/if_stage.sv`.
- Removed both replay banks, tag arrays, valid bits, MRU state, lookup/fill control, registered replay candidate state, and bypass logic.
- `fetch_ready` now follows `imem_ready` directly, and `fetch_instr` is the live `imem_data` only when `imem_ready` is high.
- Preserved redirect priority over stalls, PC hold on stalls, invalid NOP output on flush/imem stall, and static backward-branch/direct-JAL prediction from the live instruction word.
- Updated replay-specific comments in `rtl/core_pkg.sv`, `rtl/hazard_unit.sv`, and one stale execute-stage comment.

## Deviations

- No functional deviation from the hypothesis. I also removed stale documentation references outside the advisory files so the RTL comments match the new fetch behavior.

## Concerns

- CoreMark/MHz is expected to drop under randomized imem stalls because IF no longer supplies cached instruction words during `io_imemReady=0`.
- The direct worktree invocation of `bash formal/run_all.sh` could not stage files because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a shared checkout outside this sandbox's writable roots. The failure happened before any formal check ran.

## Verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- `bash formal/run_all.sh`: blocked in the worktree by sandbox permissions while removing staged files under the symlinked `formal/riscv-formal`.
- Mirrored the needed formal tree under `/tmp/auto-arch-formal-check`, symlinked in the current `rtl/`, and ran the same `bash formal/run_all.sh`: pass, `105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: Stall-Only Static Frontend Predictor
motivation:
The current best core already exceeds the CoreMark performance target:
577.76 iter/s versus the required 400 iter/s. The failing axis is area, with
5944 LUT4 against the 3500 LUT4 target, so this predictor slot should spend
some performance headroom on deleting predictor-local state rather than
adding another branch table, return predictor, or target cache.

The largest predictor-owned area in the current RTL is the IF-stage
two-bank instruction replay predictor. It carries two 128-entry banks of
instruction words, high-PC tags, valid bits, per-index MRU replacement, tag
compares, fill bypass, and a registered current-PC replay candidate. This
mechanism is useful only when the external imem bus stalls and the current
instruction has already been captured. It is not required for correctness,
because the existing hazard path already holds the PC and injects a NOP
whenever fetch is unavailable.

Since the run is deficit-driven and the CoreMark margin is roughly 44%, the
most promising predictor angle is a deliberate area tradeoff: keep the cheap
static control-flow predictor that avoids loop-back redirects, but remove the
expensive imem-stall replay predictor.
hypothesis:
Delete the registered instruction replay table from `if_stage.sv` and reduce
frontend prediction to the zero-state static next-PC policy already present:
legal backward conditional branches predict taken, aligned direct JALs
predict taken, and JALR remains unpredicted until EX. With no replay table,
`fetch_ready` becomes exactly `imem_ready`; the fetch instruction is the
live imem word only on ready cycles, and otherwise the existing stall/flush
logic presents a NOP while holding the PC.

This should remove a large amount of LUT-backed storage and IF mux/compare
logic while preserving the architectural contract. The performance loss is
the re-exposed random imem stall penalty in CoreMark cosim, but that loss
should stay within the available headroom and may be partly offset by higher
Fmax from the smaller frontend. The intended result is a strict improvement
on the currently failing LUT4 axis without crossing below the 400 iter/s
CoreMark target.

Do not change decoder legality, branch/JAL prediction semantics, redirect
priority, pipeline depth, regfile behavior, memory ordering, pending-store
behavior, divider protocol, RVFI NRET=2 ports, single-retire channel-0
convention, channel-1 tie-off, or any evaluation contract files.

changes.description:
Remove the two-bank 128-entry replay predictor: delete the replay
valid/tag/instruction arrays, MRU bits, fill-bank selection, tag-compare
lookup, registered replay candidate, replay-current-hit path, and
fill-bypass logic. Drive `fetch_ready` directly from `imem_ready` and
select `imem_data` only when ready, otherwise NOP under the existing
flush/stall behavior. Preserve redirect priority over stall, reset PC,
static backward-branch prediction, aligned direct-JAL prediction,
JALR-unpredicted behavior, `predicted_taken`, `predicted_target`, and
PC update semantics.

Update the IF/ID bundle comments to remove the stale replay-predictor
description. Keep the actual `if_id_t` and `id_ex_t` fields unchanged,
including `predicted_taken` and `predicted_target`, so the EX-stage
misprediction comparison and RVFI-facing pipeline payload do not change
structurally.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## What changed

- Removed the IF-stage two-bank replay predictor from `rtl/if_stage.sv`, including replay valid/tag/instruction arrays, MRU replacement state, lookup/fill selection, registered replay candidate, current-PC replay hit, and fill bypass logic.
- Changed IF fetch availability to `fetch_ready = imem_ready`.
- Changed IF instruction selection to use the live `imem_data` only when `imem_ready` is high; otherwise IF presents `NOP`, with the existing hazard path holding the PC and flushing the invalid fetch.
- Preserved the existing static prediction policy for legal backward conditional branches and aligned direct JALs. JALR remains unpredicted and resolves in EX.
- Updated stale replay-predictor comments in `rtl/core_pkg.sv` and the effective fetch-ready comment in `rtl/hazard_unit.sv`.

## Deviations

- No functional deviations from the hypothesis. The only extra RTL edit was a comment update in `rtl/hazard_unit.sv` so the fetch-ready contract no longer references replay hits.

## Concerns

- The expected performance tradeoff remains: imem stall cycles now always inject bubbles instead of replaying a previously captured instruction. This is intentional for LUT reduction.
- No structural pipeline, decoder, redirect-priority, RVFI, regfile, memory-ordering, pending-store, or divider protocol changes were made.

## Local verification

- Lint: `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv` passed.
- Formal: running `bash formal/run_all.sh` directly in this worktree failed before any RTL checks because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a shared tree outside the writable sandbox, and the script's staging cleanup hit `rm: ... Operation not permitted`.
- Formal self-check was rerun with the same `formal/run_all.sh` from a temporary writable staging root at `/tmp/stall-only-formal.QfwRAC`, using the modified `rtl/` and a writable riscv-formal `cores/` directory. Result: `Formal: 105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: Outcome-Only Prediction Metadata
motivation:
The current best design already clears the CoreMark throughput target:
503.87 iter/s versus the required 400 iter/s. The only failing axis is
area, with 3566 LUT4 against the 3500 LUT4 target. The remaining gap is only
about 66 LUT4, so this micro_opt slot should avoid changing the pipeline
schedule or prediction policy and instead remove redundant logic from a hot
control path.

The frontend currently carries both `predicted_taken` and a 32-bit
`predicted_target` through IF/ID and ID/EX. For the existing predictor, the
target is deterministic from the instruction and PC: predicted backward
branches use `pc + B-imm`, direct JAL uses `pc + J-imm`, and not-predicted
control flow means `pc + 4`. EX already recomputes those same branch and JAL
targets for architectural `pc_wdata`, trap handling, and redirect repair.
The carried 32-bit target therefore mostly buys a mux, compare, and pipeline
payload that can be replaced by the one-bit prediction outcome.
hypothesis:
Remove the `predicted_target` field from `if_id_t` and `id_ex_t`, keeping
only `predicted_taken` as pipeline metadata. IF should still compute the
same internal target to update the fetch PC for legal backward conditional
branches and aligned direct JALs, and JALR should remain unpredicted. The
instruction stream and all existing predictor decisions must be unchanged.

In EX, repair using outcome-only logic. Conditional branches redirect when
the resolved `branch_taken` bit differs from `in.predicted_taken`. Direct
JAL redirects only when it was not predicted taken. JALR remains an
unconditional EX-resolved redirect. Misaligned branch or jump targets still
mark the instruction as trapping, suppress redirects, clear JAL/JALR
register writes, and report `pc + 4` as the resolved next PC exactly as
today.

This should remove a 32-bit ID/EX payload, a 32-bit predicted-next-PC mux,
and the wide target equality compare from EX without changing CoreMark
cycles. The intended result is a small LUT4 and FF reduction that closes the
current 1.9% area miss, with fitness flat or slightly higher from a simpler
EX control path. Top-level IO, RVFI NRET=2 fields, single-retire channel-0
convention, channel-1 tie-off, strict `rvfi_order`, decoder illegal
defaults, memory ordering, pending-store behavior, divider protocol, and
timing-marker behavior remain unchanged.

changes.description:
Remove the 32-bit `predicted_target` member from `if_id_t` and
`id_ex_t`. Keep `predicted_taken` and update the bundle comments to
describe outcome-only static prediction metadata. Do not change any
RVFI-facing bundle fields, ALU constants, control bits, or top-level IO
assumptions.

Preserve the current internal static next-PC predictor, including
backward-branch prediction, aligned direct-JAL prediction, JALR
unpredicted behavior, replay-table live/replay instruction selection,
redirect priority, stall behavior, and `fetch_ready` semantics. Stop
assigning `out.predicted_target`; continue assigning `out.predicted_taken`.

Stop latching `predicted_target` into the ID/EX register. Leave decode,
immediate generation, register-file read addresses, operand capture,
stall/flush handling, valid propagation, and all control fields
unchanged.

Replace the carried predicted-target comparison with outcome-only
redirect repair: branches compare resolved taken/not-taken against
`in.predicted_taken`, direct JAL redirects only when not predicted, and
JALR keeps the existing EX redirect. Preserve misaligned-target trap
gating, `pc_next` generation, divider launch/completion, operand
forwarding, JAL/JALR return-data routing, EX/MEM payload semantics, and
all stall behavior.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## Changes

- Removed the 32-bit `predicted_target` member from `if_id_t` and `id_ex_t`.
- Kept IF's internal static target calculation intact for backward branches and aligned direct JALs, but stopped exporting that target in the IF/ID bundle.
- Stopped ID from latching a prediction target into ID/EX.
- Replaced EX target-equality repair with outcome-only repair:
  - conditional branches redirect when resolved `branch_taken` differs from `in.predicted_taken`;
  - direct JAL redirects only when `in.predicted_taken` is false;
  - JALR remains an unconditional EX-resolved redirect.
- Preserved the existing misaligned branch/jump trap gating, including redirect suppression, JAL/JALR write suppression, and `pc + 4` as the trapped instruction's resolved next PC.

## Deviations

- No functional deviations from the hypothesis. The local `predicted_target` signal remains inside `if_stage.sv` because fetch still needs the same internal next-PC target to preserve the existing prediction policy and instruction stream.

## Concerns

- No RTL concerns found. The change is limited to prediction metadata transport and EX redirect repair.
- Running `bash formal/run_all.sh` directly in the worktree failed before generating checks because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a shared directory outside the writable sandbox, and the script stages files under that target. I therefore ran the same script from a `/tmp` mirror containing the current RTL and riscv-formal framework so generated proof artifacts stayed writable.

## Verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- `bash formal/run_all.sh` in `/tmp/auto-arch-formal-8076`: pass, `Formal: 105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: Collapse Pending Store Slot
motivation:
The current design already clears the CoreMark throughput target with
503.87 iter/s versus the required 400 iter/s. The only failing axis is area:
3566 LUT4 versus the 3500 LUT4 target, a gap of just 66 LUT4. This structural
slot should therefore trade a small amount of store-stall hiding for a
concrete reduction in backend state and dmem arbitration logic.

The existing one-entry pending-store slot is useful under random dmem stalls,
but it is now a relatively expensive luxury for the target mix. It carries a
69-bit store payload, valid/drain/enqueue control, a live-versus-slot dmem
port mux, and extra MEM-stage predicates that let normal-RAM stores retire
before the physical write drains. Prior store-buffer experiments showed this
mechanism is a modest cycle optimization, not a large CoreMark dependency.
With more than 25% performance headroom over the target, deleting the slot is
the most direct structural way to close the remaining LUT deficit.
hypothesis:
Remove the pending-store retirement slot and collapse the data-memory
backend to a single live MEM-stage request path. All legal stores, including
aligned normal-RAM stores, should now wait in MEM until `io_dmemReady` is
high, exactly like loads and nonbufferable stores already do. When the bus is
ready, stores retire through the normal MEM/WB boundary with the same RVFI
memory address, write mask, write data, trap, and order semantics as today.
When the bus is not ready, `mem_wait` holds IF/ID/EX and clears MEM/WB valid
so the store cannot retire or write externally twice.

This deletes `store_slot.sv`, the top-level store-slot drain priority mux,
the enqueue-side store payload/control wires, and the MEM-stage special case
that allowed bufferable stores to retire while `io_dmemReady` was low. The
resulting topology is smaller and easier to place: one memory requester, one
dmem address/data/mask source, and one precise wait predicate for loads and
stores.

The expected performance cost is limited to re-exposing random dStall cycles
on stores that previously enqueued into the slot. CoreMark should remain
above 400 iter/s because the current run has substantial throughput margin,
and the simpler dmem path may recover some Fmax. The intended win is moving
LUT4 below 3500 without changing ISA behavior, decoder legality, branch/JAL
prediction, divider interlock, register file behavior, RVFI NRET=2 ports,
channel-0-only single retirement, channel-1 tie-off, or the top-level IO.

changes.description:
Delete the one-entry pending-store slot module, or leave only an unused
stub if the implementer needs a transitional lint-safe step before
removing the instantiation. No store payload should remain buffered after
this change.

Remove the store-slot instance, enqueue/drain wires, and dmem arbiter.
Drive `io_dmemAddr`, `io_dmemWData`, `io_dmemWEn`, and `io_dmemREn`
directly from the live MEM-stage outputs on every cycle. Preserve the
core module IO, RVFI channel layout, strict channel-0 order counter, and
channel-1 tie-off exactly.

Remove `store_slot_valid`, enqueue outputs, and bufferable-store
completion logic. Make every legal memory access wait for the live dmem
handshake: loads wait while `!dmem_ready`, stores wait while
`!dmem_ready`, and non-memory or trapping/misaligned instructions do not
wait. Keep byte/halfword write replication, load extraction, misalignment
trap marking, RVFI aligned memory fields, and MEM/WB hold behavior
unchanged.

Keep the existing single `mem_wait` contract, but update comments and any
now-stale assumptions so MEM wait means a live load or store is waiting
for `io_dmemReady`. Preserve load-use, imem stall, redirect, EX-long,
EX/MEM hold, and MEM/WB valid-clear behavior.

Update only comments that describe the backend memory topology so they no
longer mention a pending-store slot. Do not change pipeline bundle
widths, architectural state semantics, ALU encodings, decoder legality
defaults, RVFI fields, or the NRET=2 contract.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## What changed

- Removed the one-entry pending store slot module (`rtl/store_slot.sv`).
- Removed the `store_slot` instance, enqueue/drain wires, and dmem arbiter from `rtl/core.sv`.
- Connected `mem_stage` dmem outputs directly to the top-level dmem IO, leaving a single live MEM-stage request path.
- Simplified `rtl/mem_stage.sv` so every valid, legal, aligned load or store waits in MEM while `dmem_ready` is low.
- Kept misaligned or illegal memory operations non-waiting; they still suppress dmem access and retire as traps through the existing MEM/WB path.
- Updated stale `mem_wait` comments in `rtl/core.sv`, `rtl/mem_stage.sv`, and `rtl/hazard_unit.sv`.

## Deviations

- No functional deviation from the hypothesis.
- The direct `bash formal/run_all.sh` invocation in this sandbox could not update the protected `formal/riscv-formal/cores/auto-arch-researcher` staging directory. I reran the unmodified script from a dereferenced temporary copy under `/tmp` using the current `rtl/` contents and the same wrapper/config.

## Concerns

- No known correctness concerns. Stores now expose dmem backpressure directly, so random dStall performance should drop for store-heavy windows as expected.

## Local verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- `bash formal/run_all.sh`: blocked in-place by sandbox write permissions before checks ran.
- Temporary dereferenced copy, same script/config/RTL: pass (`Formal: 105 passed, 0 failed`).

title: Outcome-Only Static Prediction Metadata
motivation:
The current selected design already meets the CoreMark throughput target:
503.87 iter/s versus the required 400 iter/s. The only failing axis is
area, with 3566 LUT4 versus the 3500 LUT4 target. This predictor slot
should therefore avoid adding predictor capacity or accuracy state and
instead remove predictor-local logic that is redundant for the current
frontend policy.

The IF stage currently predicts only deterministic targets: legal backward
conditional branches use `pc + B-imm`, aligned direct JALs use `pc + J-imm`,
and JALR is not predicted. Nevertheless, the pipeline carries both a
one-bit `predicted_taken` and a 32-bit `predicted_target` into EX, then
compares the resolved next PC against that carried 32-bit target. For this
predictor, the carried target is redundant. When a branch or direct JAL was
predicted taken, EX recomputes the exact same target from the instruction
and PC; when it was not predicted, the predicted next PC is simply `pc+4`.

Removing only the target payload is a better area bet than removing or
shrinking the replay predictor: earlier replay-table deletion exposed too
much iStall penalty while saving little LUT. This hypothesis keeps the
successful instruction replay behavior and static control-flow policy but
trims 32 bits of prediction metadata, target muxing, fanout, and the
resolved-vs-predicted 32-bit equality compare.
hypothesis:
Remove `predicted_target` from `if_id_t` and `id_ex_t` while retaining the
existing one-bit `predicted_taken` metadata. The IF stage keeps its current
next-PC policy and replay predictor exactly intact: live imem data wins
when ready, replay hits may supply the current instruction during imem
stalls, legal backward branches and aligned direct JALs predict taken, and
JALR waits for EX.

In EX, repair predictions using outcome-only logic. For conditional
branches, redirect when the resolved `branch_taken` value differs from
`in.predicted_taken`; if both are taken, the target is necessarily the
already-computed `branch_target`. For direct JAL, redirect only if
`in.predicted_taken` is false; a correctly predicted direct JAL needs no EX
redirect because IF already fetched from `jump_target`. JALR remains
unpredicted and always redirects from EX when valid. Existing misaligned
branch/jump target trap handling remains authoritative and continues to
suppress redirects for trapped control-flow instructions.

CoreMark cycles should remain unchanged because no fetch PC decision,
replay hit/miss behavior, branch direction rule, or direct-JAL rule changes.
The expected win is a small but targeted LUT4 reduction, enough to attack
the current 66-LUT area deficit, with Fmax flat or slightly improved from
deleting a 32-bit compare and target-metadata routing. Do not alter decoder
legality, RVFI fields, retirement order, memory ordering, pending-store
behavior, divider protocol, register-file behavior, top-level IO, or the
channel-1 tie-off.

changes.description:
Remove the 32-bit `predicted_target` field from `if_id_t` and
`id_ex_t`, but keep `predicted_taken`. Update comments to describe
outcome-only prediction metadata. Do not change control encodings,
decoder legality defaults, RVFI field widths, top-level IO assumptions,
or the NRET=2 contract.

Preserve the existing static branch/direct-JAL predictor and two-bank
registered instruction replay predictor for choosing `next_pc`, but
stop assigning `out.predicted_target`. Continue assigning
`out.predicted_taken`, keep live-imem priority, replay candidate
guarding, fill bypass, MRU replacement, redirect priority, replay-miss
PC hold behavior, and `fetch_ready` semantics unchanged.

Stop latching `predicted_target` into ID/EX while preserving the
`predicted_taken` bit. Leave decode, immediate generation, register-file
read addresses, stall/flush behavior, valid propagation, and all
operand/control fields unchanged.

Delete `predicted_next_pc` and the comparison against the carried
32-bit predicted target. For branches, assert mispredict when resolved
`branch_taken` differs from `in.predicted_taken`. For direct JAL,
redirect only when not predicted taken; keep JALR EX-resolved. Preserve
misaligned-target trap gating, divider behavior, operand forwarding,
JAL/JALR return-data routing, `pc_next` generation, and EX/MEM
valid/stall behavior.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## What changed

- Removed the 32-bit `predicted_target` field from `if_id_t` and `id_ex_t`.
- Kept the IF static branch/direct-JAL prediction policy and replay predictor behavior intact.
- Stopped assigning and latching prediction targets through IF/ID and ID/EX.
- Changed EX repair logic to use outcome-only metadata:
  - Branches redirect when resolved `branch_taken` differs from `in.predicted_taken`.
  - Direct JAL redirects only when it was not predicted taken.
  - JALR remains EX-resolved and redirects when valid.
- Preserved misaligned control-flow trap gating, including redirect suppression on trapped targets.

## Deviations

- No functional deviations from the hypothesis.
- IF still computes local branch/JAL targets for its own next-PC decision; only the carried pipeline payload was removed.

## Concerns

- No known RTL concerns. The change relies on the existing invariant that IF only predicts deterministic aligned backward branches and aligned direct JAL targets.
- The direct `bash formal/run_all.sh` invocation in this sandbox could not restage files because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a protected shared tree. I reran the same script from a dereferenced `/tmp` copy so the generated formal core directory was writable.

## Local verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- `bash formal/run_all.sh`: pass in dereferenced `/tmp` copy, `105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: Single-Product RV32M Multiply
motivation:
The current selected design already meets both tournament targets:
496.63 CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3458 LUT4 versus the
3500 target. In this Pareto phase, the right micro_opt is therefore a
local "free win" that improves one axis without changing the pipeline
schedule, memory behavior, branch policy, or RVFI contract.

The ALU still describes three independent 64-bit multiply expressions in
the real RV32M path: unsigned*unsigned, signed*signed, and signed*unsigned.
Only one multiply result can ever be selected for an instruction, MUL's low
half is independent of signedness, and the signed high halves can be derived
exactly from one unsigned product with narrow correction subtracts. If the
Gowin/Yosys flow is not perfectly sharing the three multiplier operators,
this is one of the few remaining single-file changes with meaningful LUT,
DSP-routing, and Fmax upside.

This angle is narrower than the prior area-closing register-file and
prediction-metadata work already present in the selected RTL. It does not
spend any CoreMark cycles or add state; it only makes the existing one-cycle
multiply datapath cheaper to synthesize.
hypothesis:
Rewrite only the non-`RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS` multiply datapath in
`rtl/alu.sv` so it computes a single unsigned 64-bit product:

`mul_uu = {32'b0, a} * {32'b0, b}`.

Use that product directly for MUL and MULHU. Derive the signed high-half
variants from the unsigned high half with modulo-32-bit correction terms:

`mulh_hi = mul_uu[63:32] - (a[31] ? b : 32'b0) - (b[31] ? a : 32'b0)`.

`mulhsu_hi = mul_uu[63:32] - (a[31] ? b : 32'b0)`.

These identities are bit-exact for RV32M because a negative signed operand
can be represented as its unsigned value minus 2^32; any 2^64 term is
outside the retained 64 product bits. The ALTOPS substitutions must stay
unchanged, and DIV/REM must continue to return zero from `alu.sv` because
`div_unit.sv` owns those results.

CoreMark cycles should remain unchanged: MUL/MULH/MULHSU/MULHU still
complete in the same EX cycle, and no forwarding, hazard, branch, replay,
memory, divider, or retirement rule changes. The intended Pareto win is
lower LUT4 and/or higher Fmax from eliminating two explicit 32x32 multiply
descriptions and their routing, while preserving the already-met area
target and all RVFI invariants.

changes.description:
In the real RV32M multiply path, remove the explicit signed*signed and
signed*unsigned product signals. Keep one unsigned 64-bit product and
add 32-bit correction wires for MULH and MULHSU. Select the corrected
high halves in the existing M-extension result mux. Preserve the ALU
module interface, ALU op encodings, RV32I simple result path,
`RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS` formulas, DIV/REM zero placeholders, and
one-cycle combinational multiply latency. No other RTL files should
need behavioral changes.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## What changed

  - `MUL` uses `mul_uu[31:0]`
  - `MULHU` uses `mul_uu[63:32]`
  - `MULH` uses the unsigned high half minus the signed-operand correction
    terms for `a[31]` and `b[31]`

title: Prune Dead EX/MEM Branch State
motivation:
The current selected RTL meets both phase-2 targets: 496.63 CoreMark
iter/s against the 400 target, and 3458 LUT4 against the 3500 target. The
structural slot should therefore look for a free win that improves fitness
or area without spending any CoreMark cycles or adding new state.

The EX/MEM pipeline bundle still carries `branch_taken` and `branch_target`
even though no downstream stage consumes those registered copies. EX needs
the local branch outcome and target for redirect repair, misaligned-target
trap detection, and `pc_next`, but once `pc_next` has been registered the
separate branch metadata is dead. MEM/WB and RVFI use `pc_next`, `pc`,
operand values, memory metadata, and writeback data; they never read the
registered branch outcome or target.

This is a structural pipeline-register cleanup rather than another
predictor, cache, skid buffer, or store-buffer change. It removes 33 bits of
resettable EX/MEM state and the branch-target fanout feeding that state while
preserving the store-data path, memory backend, IF replay predictor, static
branch policy, divider interlock, and retirement boundary exactly. The
expected Pareto win is a small Fmax and possibly LUT4 improvement with
CoreMark cycles unchanged and no area regression.
hypothesis:
Delete `branch_taken` and `branch_target` from `ex_mem_t`. Keep the local
EX-stage `branch_taken` and `branch_target` wires exactly as they are today
for branch prediction repair, misaligned-target trap marking, redirect
target generation, and resolved `pc_next` selection. Stop assigning the
removed registered fields in the normal EX path and in the divide-complete
path.

Do not remove or merge `write_data` in this hypothesis; the store-data path
should remain bit-identical and keep its existing fanout shape. This makes
the change narrower than a general EX/MEM payload rewrite: only metadata with
no architectural or microarchitectural consumer is pruned.

CoreMark iterations/cycle should be unchanged because the instruction stream,
branch/JAL/JALR recovery, load-use interlock, MEM wait behavior,
pending-store slot, divider protocol, register file, and writeback path do
not change. RVFI remains driven from MEM/WB with the same `pc_wdata`,
source-data, memory, trap, and order semantics. Channel 0 remains the sole
single-issue retirement channel, channel 1 stays fully tied off, and the
top-level `core` IO is unchanged.

title: PC-Lockstep Replay Candidate Guard
motivation:
The current design already meets both tournament targets: 496.63 CoreMark
iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3458 LUT4 versus the 3500 target. The
accept rule is therefore strict Pareto dominance, so this predictor slot
should look for a free win: reduce predictor area or improve Fmax without
changing the CoreMark instruction stream or adding new predictor state.

The useful predictor feature in the current RTL is the two-bank registered
I-fetch replay predictor. It hides random imem stalls while keeping the
current-cycle fetch path limited to live imem data or an already registered
replay candidate. That candidate still carries a full 32-bit
`replay_cand_pc_q` and compares it against the current PC every cycle before
a replay hit can satisfy `fetch_ready`.

The PC compare is redundant if the registered candidate is maintained in
lockstep with the PC register. The IF stage already computes the next replay
lookup from exactly the PC that will be visible on the next cycle:
`redirect_target` on redirect, `next_pc` on a non-stalled fetch, and the
held `pc` on any stall. Live imem data still has priority, and fill bypass
already supplies the just-fetched word when the next lookup is the same word.
Removing the full candidate-PC register and equality check should trim the
replay predictor's hit path and reduce LUT/FF use with no cycle-count change.
hypothesis:
Delete `replay_cand_pc_q` from `if_stage.sv` and treat
`replay_cand_valid_q` as the complete replay-hit guard. `fetch_ready` should
become `imem_ready || replay_cand_valid_q`, and the replay instruction mux
should use `replay_cand_instr_q` whenever imem is stalled and the candidate
valid bit is set.

Preserve the lockstep invariant explicitly: on every cycle, the next
candidate must be generated for the same PC that the PC register will hold
on the following cycle. Redirect still selects `redirect_target`; a normal
advancing fetch still selects the IF-computed `next_pc`; any load-use,
imem-miss, dmem-wait, or long-EX stall still selects the held `pc`. Reset
clears only the candidate valid bit and bank valid bits; replay tag,
instruction payload, and the remaining replacement state stay behind their
existing valid gating.

This must not change replay table contents, bank structure, MRU replacement,
live-imem priority, fill bypass, static backward-branch prediction, aligned
direct-JAL prediction, JALR EX resolution, or EX redirect repair. A replay
miss still holds the PC and injects the existing IF bubble. If the invariant
is implemented correctly, CoreMark cycles are identical, while the design
drops a 32-bit IF predictor register plus its equality tree from the
`fetch_ready` path. Top-level IO, RVFI NRET=2 ports, channel-0-only
retirement, channel-1 tie-off, strict `rvfi_order`, decoder legality,
pending-store behavior, memory traps, and the divider protocol remain
unchanged.

changes.description:
Remove the `replay_cand_pc_q` register and the `replay_cand_pc_q == pc`
equality from `replay_current_hit`. Drive replay hit/fetch readiness
from `replay_cand_valid_q` alone, while continuing to compute
`replay_lookup_pc` from redirect target, advancing `next_pc`, or held
`pc` so the candidate remains PC-lockstep. Keep both replay banks,
compressed high-PC tags, reset-cleared valid bits, resetless payload
arrays, MRU replacement, same-entry fill bypass, live-imem priority,
redirect priority, static backward-branch prediction, aligned direct-JAL
prediction, and JALR-unpredicted behavior unchanged.

Update only the IF/ID replay predictor comments to document that the
registered replay candidate is maintained in lockstep with the fetch PC,
so its valid bit is sufficient to indicate a replay hit during external
imem stalls. Do not change bundle fields, control encodings, decoder
illegal defaults, RVFI field widths, top-level IO assumptions, or the
NRET=2 contract.

implementation_notes:
PC-Lockstep Replay Candidate Guard
==================================

What changed:
- Removed `replay_cand_pc_q` from `rtl/if_stage.sv`.
- Removed the candidate-PC equality from the replay hit path.
- `fetch_ready` is now `imem_ready || replay_cand_valid_q`.
- The fetch instruction mux now uses `replay_cand_instr_q` whenever live imem is stalled and the registered candidate valid bit is set.
- Kept the next replay lookup aligned with the next fetch PC: redirect target on redirect, predicted/sequential `next_pc` on advance, and held `pc` on stalls.
- Narrowed the internal replay lookup PC signal to word-address bits `[31:2]`, since the table uses only index/tag bits after removing the full-PC compare.
- Left replay tag/instruction payload arrays resetless and stopped resetting `replay_mru`; reset clears the candidate valid bit and both bank valid arrays.
- Updated `rtl/core_pkg.sv` comments to document that the registered replay candidate is maintained in lockstep with the fetch PC.

Deviations:
- No architectural or bundle fields were changed.
- No predictor table contents, bank structure, MRU update policy, fill bypass, live-imem priority, branch/JAL prediction, JALR handling, redirect repair, top-level IO, RVFI wiring, decoder behavior, memory behavior, or divider protocol were changed.

title: Case-Mux Memory Lane Select
motivation:
The current selected design already meets both phase-2 targets: 496.63
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3458 LUT4 versus the 3500
target. The accept rule is now strict Pareto dominance, so this micro_opt
slot should look for a local strength reduction that keeps CoreMark cycles
unchanged while trimming LUT4 or improving Fmax.

The MEM stage still expresses byte-lane selection with general variable
shifts: store/load masks use shifted 4-bit constants, and load extraction
right-shifts the full 32-bit memory word by `addr[1:0] * 8` before
sign/zero extension. The address selector is only two bits wide and the
legal cases are fixed by RV32I alignment rules, so a small explicit lane mux
is a better fit for FPGA LUTs than a synthesized barrel-shift shape.

This is narrower than changing the memory protocol, store slot, branch
predictor, register file, multiplier, or pipeline bundles. It should not
affect instruction count, stall behavior, RVFI memory reporting, or timing
markers; it only rewrites the combinational byte/halfword lane logic already
inside `rtl/mem_stage.sv`.
hypothesis:
Replace the MEM-stage variable load shifter with explicit byte and halfword
lane muxes. For byte loads, select one of `dmem_rdata[7:0]`,
`dmem_rdata[15:8]`, `dmem_rdata[23:16]`, or `dmem_rdata[31:24]` from
`in.alu_result[1:0]`. For halfword loads, select `dmem_rdata[15:0]` when
`in.alu_result[1]` is zero and `dmem_rdata[31:16]` when it is one; halfword
accesses with `addr[0] = 1` are already trapped and gated from architectural
writeback by the existing misalignment logic.

Similarly strength-reduce the byte-lane write/read mask generation. Keep
byte masks as the four one-hot cases selected by `addr[1:0]`, make halfword
masks `4'b0011` or `4'b1100` from `addr[1]`, and keep word masks `4'b1111`.
Preserve the existing `mem_access` gating so misaligned or illegal memory
operations still report zero RVFI masks and do not drive dmem enables.

CoreMark iterations/cycle should be identical because no pipeline state,
handshake, forwarding path, load-use interlock, pending-store behavior,
branch prediction, divider protocol, or writeback rule changes. The intended
Pareto win is a small LUT4 reduction and possibly better MEM/WB-to-WB timing
from replacing a generic 32-bit variable shifter and shifted mask operators
with fixed 4:1 and 2:1 muxes. Top-level IO, RVFI NRET=2 fields, channel-0
single retirement, channel-1 tie-off, strict `rvfi_order`, decoder legality,
memory traps, and CoreMark marker behavior remain unchanged.

changes.description:
Replace `shifted = dmem_rdata >> (in.alu_result[1:0] * 8)` with explicit
byte and halfword lane muxes, then feed the existing sign/zero-extension
logic from those selected lanes. Also replace the shifted 4-bit
byte-mask expressions with fixed one-hot byte cases and `addr[1]` based
halfword masks. Keep store-data replication, misalignment trap marking,
`mem_access` gating, pending-store enqueue behavior, dmem wait logic,
MEM/WB register fields, RVFI aligned-memory fields, and all module ports
unchanged.

implementation_notes:

- Replaced the MEM-stage shifted byte/halfword load extraction with explicit lane muxes in `rtl/mem_stage.sv`.
- Replaced shifted byte-mask generation with fixed byte one-hot cases, `addr[1]`-selected halfword masks, and the existing word mask.
- Kept store-data replication, misalignment detection, `mem_access` gating, store-slot behavior, MEM/WB fields, and module ports unchanged.

## Deviations

title: Unify EX/MEM Store Data Rail
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 496.63
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3458 LUT4 versus the 3500 target.
The structural slot should therefore look for a free win that improves
placement/Fmax or area without spending cycles, adding state, or touching the
predictor and memory-ordering features that are carrying the current score.

One consumed EX/MEM payload rail is still redundant. `ex_mem_t` carries both
`write_data` and `rs2_val`, and EX assigns both from the same post-forwarded
rs2 value on every normal instruction. MEM uses `write_data` only to form
store byte lanes, while MEM/WB and RVFI use `rs2_val` to report the source
operand. For a store, those two values are architecturally required to be the
same post-forwarded store data. Keeping both rails forces an extra 32-bit
pipeline payload and store-data fanout through the EX/MEM boundary for no
behavioral benefit.

Prior structural attempts that removed dead branch metadata or collapsed the
store slot either had no synthesis effect or spent performance. This change
is narrower: remove only a live-but-duplicated data rail and keep the store
path, pending-store slot, branch recovery, replay frontend, divider, and RVFI
retirement boundary otherwise bit-identical. CoreMark cycles should remain
unchanged; the intended Pareto win is lower routing/register pressure and a
small Fmax or LUT4 improvement with no area regression.
hypothesis:
Delete `write_data` from `ex_mem_t` and use the already carried
post-forwarded `rs2_val` as the sole store-data source in MEM. EX should
continue computing the forwarded rs2 operand exactly as today. In the normal
execute path, store that value only in `reg_q.rs2_val`; in the divide-complete
path, keep the existing captured rs2 value in `reg_q.rs2_val`. No instruction
needs a separate `write_data` field after EX.

In `mem_stage.sv`, build the byte/halfword/word replicated store data from
`in.rs2_val` instead of `in.write_data`. The same selected value must still
feed top-level `io_dmemWData`, pending-store enqueue data, RVFI
`mem_wdata`, and RVFI `rs2_rdata` via the existing MEM/WB payload. Load
extraction, memory masks, misalignment traps, normal-RAM store buffering,
dmem wait behavior, and MEM/WB hold semantics stay unchanged.

This is a structural payload unification, not a new bypass or scheduling
policy. It must not alter the instruction stream, load-use interlock,
forwarding priority, branch/JAL/JALR redirect behavior, divider protocol,
register file, pending-store slot, RVFI NRET=2 top-level ports, channel-0
single-retire convention, channel-1 tie-off, strict `rvfi_order`, decoder
legality defaults, or timing-marker behavior. The only intended visible
change is synthesis shape: one fewer consumed 32-bit EX/MEM data rail.

changes.description:
Remove the `write_data` member from `ex_mem_t` and update the bundle
comment to document that `rs2_val` is the post-forwarded source operand
used both for RVFI source reporting and for stores. Do not change
MEM/WB fields, ALU op encodings, decoder legality defaults, RVFI field
widths, top-level IO assumptions, or the NRET=2 contract.

Stop assigning the removed EX/MEM `write_data` field in both the normal
execute path and the divide-complete path. Preserve the existing
forwarded `rs2` computation and continue assigning it to `rs2_val`.
Keep operand forwarding, JAL/JALR return-data routing, branch repair,
misaligned-target trap gating, divider launch/completion, EX/MEM valid
behavior, and stall handling unchanged.

Replace all store-data uses of `in.write_data` with `in.rs2_val` before
byte/halfword replication. Use the resulting replicated value for live
dmem writes, pending-store enqueue payloads, and RVFI `mem_wdata` exactly
as today. Preserve load extraction, misalignment traps, dmem request
gating, store-slot enqueue/wait behavior, MEM/WB fields, and RVFI memory
reporting.

title: Branch-Only Prediction Metadata
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 496.63
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3458 LUT4 versus the 3500 target.
The accept rule is therefore strict Pareto dominance, so this predictor slot
should target a free win: remove predictor repair logic that cannot affect
the CoreMark instruction stream, rather than adding another direction table,
return predictor, target cache, or replay structure.

The successful prior predictor change removed the carried 32-bit predicted
target and kept only one outcome bit in IF/ID and ID/EX. After that change,
the bit is still set for both predicted conditional branches and predicted
direct JALs. EX uses it for branch taken/not-taken repair and also keeps a
direct-JAL fallback term that redirects if a non-JALR jump was not predicted
taken.

For the current IF policy, that direct-JAL fallback is redundant. A valid
aligned direct JAL entering ID is always predicted in IF because JAL has no
funct legality filter and the target is computed immediately from `pc +
J-imm`. A misaligned direct JAL is intentionally not predicted and later
traps in EX with redirect suppressed and `pc+4` as the reported next PC.
Stalled or killed fetch cycles either hold ID/EX or inject an invalid NOP, so
they cannot create a valid aligned direct JAL with missing prediction
metadata. The carried one-bit metadata is therefore only needed for
conditional branch direction.
hypothesis:
Make the remaining prediction metadata branch-only. Keep IF's actual PC
selection exactly as today: legal backward conditional branches still predict
taken, aligned direct JALs still update the fetch PC in IF, JALR remains
unpredicted, live imem data still has priority over replay data, and the
two-bank registered replay predictor is unchanged.

Change the IF/ID `predicted_taken` payload so it records only
`branch_predict_taken`, not `branch_predict_taken || jal_predict_taken`.
ID continues to latch that single bit into ID/EX. In EX, remove the
`jal_mispredict` term and make jump redirect generation JALR-only:
conditional branches still redirect when resolved `branch_taken` differs
from the carried branch prediction bit, direct JALs never need EX repair, and
JALR still redirects from EX when valid and non-trapping.

Misaligned branch and jump target handling remains authoritative. A
misaligned direct JAL or JALR still marks the retiring instruction as a trap,
suppresses redirect, clears the link write, and reports `pc+4` as `pc_next`.
This change does not alter the fetch PC sequence for any legal CoreMark
instruction, replay table contents, branch direction policy, direct-JAL
target policy, JALR policy, pipeline depth, hazard interlocks, divider
protocol, memory ordering, pending-store behavior, RVFI fields, channel-0
retirement order, channel-1 tie-off, or top-level IO.

CoreMark cycles should remain identical. The intended Pareto win is a small
LUT4 and/or Fmax improvement from deleting the direct-JAL prediction-metadata
fanout and the EX-side non-JALR jump repair term, while preserving the
already-met LUT target.

changes.description:
Keep the existing internal static predictor and two-bank replay
predictor for choosing `next_pc`, including backward conditional branch
prediction, aligned direct-JAL prediction, JALR-unpredicted behavior,
live-imem priority, replay candidate guarding, fill bypass, MRU
replacement, redirect priority, and `fetch_ready` semantics. Change only
the exported IF/ID prediction metadata so `out.predicted_taken` carries
`branch_predict_taken` alone; direct JAL prediction remains internal to
IF's next-PC choice and is no longer transported downstream.

Continue latching the single prediction bit into ID/EX without changing
decode, immediate generation, register-file read addresses, operand
capture, stall/flush behavior, or valid propagation. Treat the bit as
branch-direction metadata only in comments or signal naming if the
implementer chooses to clarify it.

Remove the direct-JAL `jal_mispredict` repair path. Branches should keep
the current outcome-only repair against `in.predicted_taken`; direct JALs
should not request an EX redirect; JALR should remain the only jump class

routing, divider launch/completion, operand forwarding, and EX/MEM

Update only the IF/ID and ID/EX prediction comments to describe

title: Power-of-Two Store Buffer Range Decode
motivation:
The current selected RTL meets both phase-2 targets: 515.21 CoreMark
iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3451 LUT4 versus the 3500 target. The
accept rule is therefore strict Pareto dominance, so this micro_opt slot
should look for a true strength reduction that keeps CoreMark cycles
unchanged while shaving LUT4 or improving Fmax.

The MEM stage still decides whether a store may retire into the pending
store slot with a general unsigned comparison:

`in.alu_result < 32'h0010_0000`.

The limit is exactly 1 MiB, a power of two. That comparison is therefore
bit-equivalent to checking that address bits `[31:20]` are all zero. The
current expression invites synthesis to build a wider subtract/compare
structure on the MEM-stage store-control path, even though only twelve
high address bits are relevant. This is a narrower and safer area target
than changing the store slot protocol, replay predictor, register file,
multiplier, or pipeline payloads.
hypothesis:
Replace the MEM-stage normal-RAM store-bufferability comparison with an
explicit high-bit decode:

`normal_ram_store_addr = (in.alu_result[31:20] == 12'b0)`.

Then drive `store_bufferable` from `mem_access && in.ctrl.mem_write &&
normal_ram_store_addr`. This is exactly equivalent to the existing
`< 32'h0010_0000` predicate for 32-bit unsigned addresses, because the
normal RAM window is `[0x00000000, 0x00100000)`. UART and timing-marker
stores around `0x10000000` remain nonbufferable, and out-of-window stores
are still treated exactly as before by the surrounding memory contract.

No pipeline state, handshake, retirement, RVFI field, byte-lane logic,
load extraction, misalignment trap, store-slot enqueue protocol, dmem
wait rule, forwarding path, branch predictor, divider protocol, decoder
legality rule, or top-level IO changes. CoreMark iterations/cycle should
be identical. The intended Pareto win is a small LUT4 reduction and
possibly a modest MEM-stage Fmax improvement from replacing a wide
constant comparator with a simple high-bit zero test.

changes.description:
Replace the `in.alu_result < NORMAL_RAM_LIMIT` store-bufferability
predicate with an explicit high-address-bit zero check for the
1 MiB normal RAM window. Keep the existing `mem_access` and
`in.ctrl.mem_write` gating, pending-store slot behavior, dmem wait
behavior, byte/halfword masks, load lane muxes, misalignment trap
marking, MEM/WB payload, RVFI aligned-memory reporting, and module
ports unchanged.

title: Prune Dead LUI Control Rail
motivation:
The current selected RTL meets both phase-2 targets: 515.21 CoreMark iter/s
against the 400 target, and 3451 LUT4 against the 3500 target. The
structural slot should therefore look for a free win: a change that preserves
CoreMark cycles and the already-tight LUT result while improving synthesis
shape on at least one axis.

The pipeline control bundle still carries `ctrl.is_lui` through ID/EX,
EX/MEM, and MEM/WB, but no downstream stage consumes it. LUI behavior is
already fully represented by `alu_op = ALU_LUI`, `alu_src = 1`, and
`reg_write = 1`; EX selects the LUI result through the normal ALU path and
WB writes it as an ordinary ALU result. A repository search shows
`ctrl.is_lui` is only assigned in ID and never read after that.

Previous structural attempts that removed wider EX/MEM data rails or changed
store buffering perturbed placement or spent cycles. This is narrower: delete
only one architecturally dead control rail from the inter-stage payloads,
while leaving the decoder's standalone LUI decode output available for the
existing decoder unit tests if desired. The expected result is a small FF,
mux, and routing reduction with identical instruction stream, stalls,
prediction, memory behavior, and RVFI retirement.
hypothesis:
Remove `is_lui` from `ctrl_t` and stop latching it into the pipeline
control payload. Keep LUI decode semantics otherwise unchanged: the decoder
must still recognize legal LUI, clear `is_illegal`, assert `reg_write`, set
`alu_src`, and drive `ALU_LUI` so the existing EX ALU path produces the
immediate value.

To keep this change isolated from test-facing decode observability, the
`decoder.sv` `is_lui` output may remain in place and continue reporting LUI
when that module is tested directly. The structural win comes from removing
the unused bit from `ctrl_t`, not from changing architectural decode. If the
implementer chooses to remove the decoder port as well, any RTL instantiation
and local cocotb tests must be updated without changing ISA behavior.

CoreMark cycles should be exactly unchanged because no PC selection,
branch/JAL/JALR repair, load-use interlock, forwarding priority, ALU
operation, divider protocol, store-slot behavior, MEM wait rule, writeback
mux, or RVFI field changes. The top-level `core` IO, RVFI NRET=2 channel
layout, channel-0-only single-retire convention, channel-1 tie-off, strict
`rvfi_order`, decoder illegal-default discipline, memory traps, and timing
marker behavior remain unchanged.

control fields, MEM/WB fields, RVFI field widths, top-level IO

title: Stall-Decoupled Static Predictor Enable
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 515.21
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3451 LUT4 versus the 3500 target.
The accept rule is now strict Pareto dominance, so this predictor slot should
look for a free win that improves Fmax or trims logic without changing the
instruction stream, replay hit rate, or area target.

The useful predictor structures are already known from the experiment log:
the two-bank registered instruction replay predictor and the static
backward-branch/direct-JAL next-PC rule. Larger direction tables, return
target predictors, skewed replay indexing, and replay replacement changes
generally lost more Fmax than they saved in cycles. The remaining promising
predictor angle is therefore a small timing cleanup inside the existing IF
prediction cone.

Today `if_stage.sv` gates `branch_predict_taken` and `jal_predict_taken`
with `predict_enable = !fetch_kill && !stall`. The `stall` term is redundant
for correctness. When IF is stalled by load-use, dmem wait, or a long EX op,
the PC register is not loaded and ID/EX either holds or flushes the IF
payload. When IF is stalled by an imem miss, the hazard unit also asserts the
IF flush input, so `fetch_kill` already suppresses a valid instruction. In
other words, the static predictor's taken result is not architecturally
consumed on stalled cycles, while the PC write enable and replay lookahead
selection already preserve the held PC.
hypothesis:
Remove the `stall` input from the IF-stage static prediction enable. The
branch and direct-JAL prediction predicates should be killed by reset, IF
flush, or EX redirect, but not by the generic pipeline stall signal. Keep the
PC register update rule exactly as today: redirect overrides everything, and
otherwise the PC loads `next_pc` only when `!stall`.

This means `branch_predict_taken` still recognizes only legal aligned
backward conditional branches, and `jal_predict_taken` still recognizes only
aligned direct JALs. JALR remains unpredicted. The two-bank replay predictor
remains unchanged, including live-imem priority, candidate-PC guard,
compressed tags, fill bypass, MRU replacement, and `fetch_ready` semantics.
Crucially, `replay_lookup_pc` must continue to use the held `pc` while
stalled; only the static predictor's local enable should stop depending on
`stall`.

CoreMark cycles should remain identical because the fetch PC sequence on
advancing cycles, replay hits and misses, branch direction policy, direct-JAL
target policy, and EX repair logic do not change. The intended Pareto win is
a small Fmax and/or LUT4 improvement from removing high-fanout stall control
from the IF branch/JAL prediction and next-PC select cone. Decoder legality,
misaligned-target traps, RVFI NRET=2 ports, channel-0 retirement ordering,
channel-1 tie-off, pending-store behavior, memory traps, divider protocol,
register-file behavior, top-level IO, and CoreMark marker behavior remain
unchanged.

changes.description:
Change the static predictor enable so branch and direct-JAL prediction
are gated by `!fetch_kill` but not by `!stall`. Preserve the current
legal-branch funct3 guard, backward-branch-taken rule, aligned direct-JAL
rule, JALR-unpredicted behavior, branch/JAL target calculations, and
`out.predicted_taken` semantics on advancing cycles. Keep redirect
priority over stalls and keep the PC register write guarded by `!stall`.
Do not change the replay table structure, replay candidate PC compare,
live-imem priority, fill bypass, MRU replacement, or `fetch_ready`
behavior.

Update only the IF/ID prediction comments if needed to clarify that
prediction metadata is meaningful only when the IF payload advances into
ID. Do not change bundle fields, control encodings, decoder illegal
defaults, RVFI field widths, top-level IO assumptions, or the NRET=2
contract.

title: Share EX PC Target Adders
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 517.86
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3449 LUT4 versus the 3500
target. The accept rule is strict Pareto dominance, so this micro_opt slot
should target a local free win that keeps the CoreMark instruction stream
and cycle count unchanged while reducing LUT4 or improving Fmax.

The EX stage still spells the same PC arithmetic several times in the
control-flow resolve cone. `in.pc + in.imm` is computed for conditional
branch targets and again for direct JAL targets. `in.pc + 32'd4` is used for
the not-taken branch next PC, JAL/JALR link data, and the default resolved
`pc_next`. These expressions sit in the branch/jump redirect and EX/MEM
payload path, which is exactly the kind of high-fanout combinational logic
where the Gowin/Yosys flow has shown sensitivity to small RTL shape changes.

This is narrower than changing the predictor, replay table, register file,
store buffering, multiplier, pipeline bundles, or RVFI retirement. It only
makes existing EX-stage arithmetic sharing explicit so synthesis is not
invited to preserve duplicate 32-bit adders or extra routing on the
control-flow path.
hypothesis:
In `rtl/ex_stage.sv`, introduce shared EX-local wires for `pc_plus4_ex =
in.pc + 32'd4` and `pc_imm_target = in.pc + in.imm`. Use `pc_imm_target`
for both `branch_target` and the direct-JAL side of `jump_target`. Use
`pc_plus4_ex` everywhere the normal execute path currently repeats
`in.pc + 32'd4`: the not-taken branch next PC, JAL/JALR link write data,
misaligned-control-flow trap `pc_next`, and default sequential `pc_next`.

Keep the JALR target adder separate because it is `rs1 + imm` and must still
clear bit 0. Keep all branch condition comparisons, prediction repair,
misaligned branch/jump target trap behavior, divider launch/completion,
operand forwarding, EX/MEM register valid/stall handling, and RVFI-visible
`pc_wdata` semantics unchanged.

CoreMark iterations/cycle should be identical because no PC decision,
branch/JAL/JALR policy, replay behavior, hazard interlock, memory protocol,
divider protocol, writeback rule, or retirement rule changes. The intended
Pareto win is a small LUT4 reduction and/or higher Fmax from replacing
repeated PC add expressions in the EX control-flow cone with explicit shared
results, while preserving the already-met LUT target and every invariant in
`CLAUDE.md`.

changes.description:

replace the repeated normal-path PC add expressions with those wires.
Use the shared target for conditional branches and direct JAL, keep the
existing `rs1 + imm` JALR sum and bit-0 clearing, and preserve branch
condition logic, redirect repair, misaligned-target trap gating,
divider behavior, forwarding, JAL/JALR return-data routing, EX/MEM
payload fields, valid/stall behavior, top-level IO, and RVFI semantics.

title: Stage-Local Control Payload Split
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 517.86
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3449 LUT4 versus the 3500 target.
The structural slot should therefore target a free win: improve Fmax or LUT4
without changing the CoreMark instruction stream, stall policy, memory
ordering, or RVFI contract.

The pipeline still carries one monolithic `ctrl_t` through every stage.
Several control fields are needed only in EX: `alu_op`, `alu_src`,
`branch_op`, `is_branch`, `is_jump`, `is_jalr`, `is_auipc`, and the dead
`is_lui` rail. After EX resolves the ALU result, branch repair, jump target,
and misaligned-control-flow traps, those bits are copied into EX/MEM and
MEM/WB even though MEM and WB never consume them. MEM/WB then carries still
more unused memory-control detail even though WB/RVFI only need final
write-enable, load-result select, and trap status.

Prior structural experiments that removed a single dead rail or a duplicated
data rail were too small and placement-noisy, while collapsing the store slot
spent real cycles. This hypothesis keeps all successful structures intact and
removes a larger coherent class of dead inter-stage payload: stage-inappropriate
control bits. The intended win is less resettable pipeline state, less
control fanout, and a simpler MEM/WB writeback cone with identical cycles.
hypothesis:
Split the current all-stage `ctrl_t` into stage-local control payloads. Keep
the ID/EX control view rich enough for EX and hazard detection, but project it
down at the EX/MEM boundary to only the fields MEM still needs:
memory read/write, width, sign-extension, register write, load-result select,
and illegal/trap status. Project it down again at MEM/WB to only the fields WB
and RVFI still need: register write, load-result select, and illegal/trap
status.

EX must continue to use the original decode information for operand muxing,
branch comparison, JAL/JALR target generation, divider launch, and
misaligned-target trap handling. When EX creates the EX/MEM payload, it should
build the smaller MEM-stage control struct explicitly, applying the existing
misaligned-control-flow trap modification by setting `is_illegal` and clearing
`reg_write`. The divide-complete path must make the same projection from the
saved ID/EX control.

MEM should consume only the reduced EX/MEM control struct for dmem access,
store-slot enqueue, wait generation, byte-lane selection, load extraction, and
memory misalignment traps. When MEM creates MEM/WB, it should build the final
writeback/RVFI control struct explicitly, preserving the existing behavior
that memory misalignment sets trap status and clears register writeback.

This is a structural payload split, not a scheduling or protocol change. Do
not alter branch/JAL/JALR prediction or repair, replay-table behavior,
load-use interlocks, forwarding priority, pending-store behavior, divider
protocol, register-file behavior, memory masks/data, RVFI NRET=2 ports,
channel-0-only single retirement, channel-1 tie-off, strict `rvfi_order`,
decoder illegal-default discipline, top-level IO, or CoreMark timing markers.
CoreMark cycles should be unchanged; the expected Pareto win is lower LUT4
and/or higher Fmax from removing unused control payload and fanout.

changes.description:
Replace the single all-stage `ctrl_t` payload with stage-local control
structs, or keep `ctrl_t` as the ID/EX view and add narrower EX/MEM and
MEM/WB control structs. EX/MEM should not carry EX-only fields such as
ALU op/source, branch op, branch/jump class, AUIPC, or LUI. MEM/WB should
carry only final writeback/trap control. Preserve all existing pipeline
data fields, RVFI-facing fields, ALU encodings, decoder legality
assumptions, top-level IO assumptions, and the NRET=2 contract.

Populate the ID/EX control view from the decoder as today, but stop
latching any field that is not part of the chosen ID/EX control struct.
The decoder's standalone `is_lui` output may remain for direct decoder
tests, but LUI pipeline behavior should continue to be represented by
`ALU_LUI`, `alu_src`, and `reg_write`. Preserve immediate generation,
register-file reads, stall/flush behavior, valid propagation, and the
load-use-visible `mem_read` bit.

Project the rich ID/EX control into the narrower EX/MEM control payload
on both the normal execute path and the divide-complete path. Preserve the
existing local EX use of ALU, branch, jump, AUIPC, divider, and prediction
metadata, including misaligned-target trap handling and redirect
suppression. Apply trap modifications to the projected EX/MEM control by
setting `is_illegal` and clearing `reg_write` exactly as today.

Consume the narrower EX/MEM control for memory access, store-slot enqueue,
dmem wait, byte/halfword masks, load extraction, and memory misalignment
detection. Build the final MEM/WB control payload explicitly from the MEM
result, preserving the existing behavior for illegal instructions,
misaligned memory traps, load-result selection, and register-write
suppression. Do not change dmem port behavior, store-slot ordering,
MEM/WB data fields, or RVFI memory reporting.

Update the writeback mux and comments to use the final MEM/WB control
payload. Preserve the current write enable condition, x0 write dropping in
the regfile, load-versus-ALU result selection, and RVFI `rd_wdata`
semantics.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## Changes

- Split the monolithic pipeline control payload into stage-local structs:
  - `ctrl_t` remains the rich ID/EX view for EX operand muxing, branch/jump handling, divider launch, hazard detection, and memory projection.
  - `mem_ctrl_t` is carried in EX/MEM and contains only memory access, writeback select, register write, and trap control.
  - `wb_ctrl_t` is carried in MEM/WB and contains only register write, load-result select, and trap control.
- Removed the dead `is_lui` rail from the ID/EX control payload. The decoder `is_lui` output is still present and connected to an explicitly unused local signal so decoder-level tests can continue to observe it.
- Updated EX to explicitly project `ctrl_t` into `mem_ctrl_t` on both the normal execute path and the divide-complete path. Misaligned branch/jump target traps still set trap status and clear register writeback before entering EX/MEM.
- Updated MEM to consume `mem_ctrl_t` for dmem access, masks, load extraction, wait generation, store-slot enqueue, and memory misalignment detection. MEM now explicitly builds `wb_ctrl_t`, preserving the existing memory-misalignment trap behavior.
- Updated WB comments and mux usage to reflect the final MEM/WB control payload.

## Deviations

title: Normal-RAM Scoped Replay Tags
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 517.86
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3449 LUT4 versus the 3500
target. With both axes satisfied, this predictor slot needs a free win:
reduce predictor area or improve IF timing without changing CoreMark cycles
or weakening the architectural contract.

The front end's useful predictor state is the two-bank registered replay
table that hides random imem stalls. Removing it exposed too much iStall
penalty, so the better predictor angle is to keep the replay behavior for
the benchmark while shrinking metadata that does not need to cover the full
32-bit address space. The CoreMark image executes from the normal low RAM
region below 0x00100000, and the UART/timing-marker region is data MMIO, not
an instruction source. Today each replay entry still stores and compares
pc[31:9], a 23-bit tag, even though low-RAM instruction replay only needs
pc[19:9] once pc[31:20] is known to be zero.

A safe compression is to scope the replay predictor to the normal-RAM
instruction window and fall back to the live imem handshake for any PC
outside that window. This preserves correctness for arbitrary branch targets
because high-address fetches simply stop using replay hits; it preserves
CoreMark cycles because the benchmark instruction stream remains in low RAM.
hypothesis:
Compress the replay table tag from full high PC bits to low-RAM tag bits.
Store only pc[19:9] in each replay bank and require pc[31:20] == 0 for both
fills and lookup hits. If the current or lookahead PC is outside the normal
1 MiB RAM window, do not allocate a replay entry and force the registered
replay candidate invalid; the fetch path then behaves like a normal imem
stall until io_imemReady supplies the instruction.

Keep the full 32-bit replay candidate PC guard for the current-PC hit check.
That guard, plus the high-address lookup disable, prevents compressed-tag
aliasing from ever supplying an instruction for the wrong architectural PC.
For low-RAM PCs, the replay index, MRU replacement, fill bypass, live-imem
priority, candidate registration, static backward-branch prediction,
aligned direct-JAL prediction, JALR EX resolution, and EX redirect repair
should be bit-identical to the current design.

CoreMark iterations/cycle should remain unchanged because all fetched
benchmark instructions are replayable low-RAM addresses and the PC sequence
is unchanged. The intended Pareto win is lower LUT4 and possibly higher Fmax
from cutting each replay tag comparator and tag RAM from 23 bits to 11 bits,
while leaving top-level IO, RVFI NRET=2 ports, channel-0 single retirement,
channel-1 tie-off, strict rvfi_order, decoder legality, memory traps,
pending-store behavior, and the divider protocol unchanged.

changes.description:
Scope the replay predictor to the low 1 MiB normal-RAM instruction
window. Change the replay tag constants so bank tags store pc[19:9]
instead of pc[31:9]. Add explicit low-address guards for the fill PC and
the lookahead lookup PC: only allocate entries when pc[31:20] is zero,
and only report bank hits when replay_lookup_pc[31:20] is zero. When the
lookup PC is outside that window, drive the next registered replay
candidate invalid so fetch_ready can only come from live imem_ready.
Preserve the full 32-bit replay_cand_pc_q equality guard, live-imem
priority, fill bypass for same low-RAM entry fills, MRU replacement,
two-bank structure, redirect priority, static backward-branch prediction,
aligned direct-JAL prediction, JALR-unpredicted behavior, and all PC
update semantics.

Update only the IF/ID replay predictor comments to document that the
replay table is optimized for the low 1 MiB normal-RAM instruction
region, with compressed pc[19:9] tags guarded by a pc[31:20] zero check.
Do not change bundle fields, control encodings, decoder illegal
defaults, RVFI field widths, top-level IO assumptions, or the NRET=2
contract.

title: Resetless Divider Payload State
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 517.86
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3449 LUT4 versus the 3500
target. The accept rule is strict Pareto dominance, so this micro_opt slot
should target a local free win that keeps CoreMark cycles unchanged while
reducing LUT4 or improving Fmax.

The divider is deliberately cold relative to the one-cycle ALU path, but it
still resets a large amount of payload state: dividend, divisor, quotient,
remainder, count, sign flags, the result register, and the EX-stage saved
divide instruction operands. None of those payload bits are architecturally
observable after reset. The divider FSM state and the EX-side active/valid
bits already guard every use of the payload, and each divide launch
overwrites the saved operands and all datapath state before the result can be
consumed.

This is the same resetless-valid-mask principle that already paid off in the
register file, but scoped to the long-latency divide datapath. It avoids the
riskier timing-sensitive changes from prior rounds: no multiplier rewrite,
no replay table changes, no branch arithmetic sharing, no store-buffer
protocol change, and no pipeline payload split.
hypothesis:
Make the divider datapath payload registers resetless while keeping the
protocol control resettable. In `div_unit.sv`, reset only the FSM state
required to make `busy=0` and `done=0` after reset. Leave `result_q`,
`dividend_q`, `divisor_q`, `quotient_q`, `remainder_q`, `count_q`,
`quot_neg_q`, `rem_neg_q`, and `want_rem_q` without reset assignments.
Every `start` path must still initialize the exact data needed by the
subsequent BUSY/DONE states before any value can influence `result`.

Apply the same rule to the EX-stage divide shadow state. Keep
`div_active_q` and `div_result_valid_q` reset to zero, because they guard
launch/completion and result replay. Leave `div_result_q`, `div_in_q`,
`div_rs1_q`, and `div_rs2_q` resetless, because they are written on
`div_start` or `div_done` and are only consumed when the active/valid guard
proves they contain a launched divide instruction.

The ALTOPS and real RV32M divide/remainder behavior must remain bit
identical, including divide-by-zero and signed-overflow results. The
multi-cycle divider latency, EX long-busy interlock, forwarding, branch
repair, memory behavior, replay predictor, register file, RVFI NRET=2 ports,
channel-0 retirement order, channel-1 tie-off, decoder illegal defaults, and
top-level IO all remain unchanged. CoreMark cycles should be identical; the
intended Pareto win is lower reset mux/fanout and a small LUT4 or Fmax
improvement with no performance regression.

changes.description:
Remove reset assignments from divide datapath payload registers while
preserving the reset of the FSM state that drives `busy` and `done`.
Ensure both the `RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS` branch and the real divider branch
assign the result/data payload on `start` before it can be observed.
Keep the state machine, divide-by-zero and signed-overflow handling,
32-iteration algorithm, ALTOPS formulas, module ports, and output
protocol unchanged.

Remove reset assignments from the EX-stage divide shadow payload
registers (`div_result_q`, `div_in_q`, `div_rs1_q`, and `div_rs2_q`)
while keeping `div_active_q` and `div_result_valid_q` reset to zero.
Preserve divide launch/completion gating, result capture, EX/MEM bubble
behavior while busy, normal EX/MEM register reset, operand forwarding,
branch/JAL/JALR handling, and all RVFI-visible semantics.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## What changed

- `rtl/div_unit.sv`: removed reset assignments from divider payload registers. Reset now only drives the divider FSM state back to `ST_IDLE`, preserving `busy=0` and `done=0` after reset. The ALTOPS and real divider start paths still assign the payload needed before any result can be observed.
- `rtl/ex_stage.sv`: removed reset assignments from EX-stage divide shadow payload registers `div_result_q`, `div_in_q`, `div_rs1_q`, and `div_rs2_q`. Kept `div_active_q` and `div_result_valid_q` reset to zero so launched/completed divide replay remains guarded.
